title: Everyday Slang
description: The casual Hindi that young Indians actually use with friends, on chats, and on the street.
situation: Hanging out, texting, and joking around with friends in relaxed, everyday Hindi.

phrases.tip: The all-purpose friendly filler. Drop it into almost any sentence with friends: 'kya yaar', 'chal yaar'. Casual, used with peers.
An exasperated or affectionate exclamation, like 'ugh, dude'. Very common when something is annoying or surprising.
Bombay slang gone national. Describe a relaxed person or just say it to mean 'it's all good'.
Made iconic by Bollywood actor Anil Kapoor. Playful and a little retro, but everyone gets it.
Hugely common. 'mast hai' means 'it's great'. Can also describe nice weather, food, or a chill mood.
From English 'time pass'. As an activity it means hanging out idly; as a verdict it means 'meh, just okay'.
Dismiss something boring or worthless: 'faltu baat' (pointless talk). Mildly cheeky, fine among friends.

phrases.english: Come on / Let's go / Alright then
phrases.tip: Literally 'walk/move'. Used to mean 'let's go', 'okay fine', or to wrap up: 'chal, baad mein baat karte hain'.
Call out talk or a movie you didn't like: 'pure bakwaas'. Cheeky but not vulgar.
Literally 'what a thing'. A warm exclamation of praise or delight. Safe with anyone.
Strong praise for something powerful or stylish, like a song or a bike. Young, energetic register.
A celebrated Indian concept: an improvised, resourceful workaround. 'koi jugaad karte hain' means 'let's figure something out'.
Say 'thak gaya' if you're male, 'thak gayi' if you're female. Everyday way to say you're wiped out.
Tease a friend who's too afraid to do something. Playful ribbing only, with close friends.
Literally 'it's correct'. The everyday way to agree or approve, like 'cool, works for me'.
Literally 'number one'. Praise something as first-class: 'khaana ek number tha' (the food was the best).
